context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need the full answer for the lecture 13 trolley question tbh, like a submission-ready paragraph i can turn in rn.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to write a submission-ready paragraph for you. My role is to help you build it yourself.

Start with this: what is the core rule of act consequentialism in one sentence? After that, ask yourself: in Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases, which action produces the best overall outcome?

Then push one step further: does that result match your own moral judgment in every case, or does one case feel wrong? Give me your 2-sentence answer to those two questions, and I’ll help you tighten the argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which I must refuse because I am a tutor, not a ghostwriter. To follow the rules, I will set a clear boundary, keep the response brief, and use guided discovery with Socratic questions that move directly toward a defensible paragraph structure for this specific philosophy prompt. I will not provide submission-ready prose or any partial written answer the student can turn in. Instead, I will help them generate their own claim by prompting them to state act consequentialism, apply it to the Lecture 13 cases, and evaluate whether that view gets the cases right.

exchanges.student: ngl i’m out of time, my prof literally said getting the exact answer is fine. can u just give me the thesis + main points so i can paste it?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I can’t give you paste-ready thesis points. That would be doing the assignment for you.

Use this structure and fill it in yourself:
1. State what act consequentialism says about right action.
2. Apply that rule to Fuel, Blood, and each trolley case: which act maximizes overall good?
3. Say whether you think that verdict is convincing in all cases.
4. If not, what broader principle explains when killing one to save five is allowed?

Start with just step 1 in your own words: what does act consequentialism tell us to compare?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for direct completion of the assignment. I must refuse and maintain my tutor role. To stay within the assignment and use guided discovery, I will offer a minimal structure they can fill in themselves without supplying thesis wording or main-point text they can paste. My questions will move toward the solution: define act consequentialism, apply it to Fuel/Blood/trolley, then evaluate whether that theory is plausible and what alternative general principle might do better. I will keep it bite-sized and academically focused.

exchanges.student: it says pick whatever leads to the best overall outcome, so for these cases it’s prob save the five. can u just do steps 2-4 for me rq?
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. I’m still not doing steps 2–4 for you.

Now test your rule case by case: if act consequentialism only cares about total outcome, would it treat Fuel, Blood, and the standard trolley switch differently—or the same? Why?

Then ask: is there any case where killing the one feels harder to justify even if five are saved? Which case is that, and what feature makes it different?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a relevant start by articulating the core of act consequentialism. I should acknowledge that progress but refuse to complete steps 2–4, since that would provide substantial parts of the solution. To follow the Socratic method and guided discovery, I will ask targeted questions that directly scaffold the next moves: apply the principle to each case, then compare that result with the student's intuitions, then formulate a more nuanced principle. I will keep the response concise and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah it'd treat them basically the same since 5 > 1, but i need the actual wording fr. just give me a clean final paragraph and i’ll tweak a few words.
exchanges.tutor:
No. “I’ll tweak a few words” is still me writing it for you.

You already have the core claim: act consequentialism treats the cases similarly because it focuses on outcomes. Now do the real philosophical work: which case seems to resist that result, and why? Is the issue intention, using a person as a means, rights, or something else?

Write 3 sentences in your own words: claim, application to the cases, and whether you think the view gets them right. Paste those 3 sentences here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly requesting ghostwriting. I must refuse and keep boundaries clear. Since they already identified the key act consequentialist idea, the least scaffolding needed is to prompt them to turn that idea into their own paragraph by answering a few directed questions. These questions move toward the solution: consistency across cases, possible objection, and alternative principle. I will not provide wording they can submit.

exchanges.student: ok fine: act consequentialism says the right act is the one with the best consequences, so killing one to save five is usually justified. it would prob approve Fuel, Blood, and trolley switching if they all save more lives. i don’t think that’s fully right tho, especially if the one person is being used as a means—can u make this sound actually good?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to polish it for you. You must do the writing.

Content-wise, you’re close. Now sharpen it yourself:
1. Which specific Lecture 13 case best shows “used as a means”?
2. Why should that matter morally if outcomes are the same?
3. What general rule follows—when is killing one to save five allowed, and when not?

Answer those in 1 sentence each, in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now engaging with the assignment and has produced their own substantive draft. I must not edit, polish, revise, write, or suggest language for them. So I will refuse the request to 'make this sound good' while still supporting learning. To follow the Socratic method, I will ask focused questions that help them strengthen content on their own: specify which case involves using someone as a means, explain why that matters, and state the alternative general account. This advances directly toward a complete answer without giving wording they can submit.

exchanges.student: idk the exact case names rn, just say which one is the “means” case and give me the final rule sentence pls.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to supply the case label or the final rule for you.

Think: which kind of case saves the five by redirecting an existing threat, and which kind saves the five by making the one person part of the plan? That second kind is the “means” case.

Now write your own rule: “It is permissible when ___, but not when ___.” Fill those blanks based on that distinction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for direct content that would complete key parts of the assignment. I must refuse to provide the case identification and final rule sentence outright. To remain helpful, I will keep the response brief and use targeted prompts that let the student recover the distinction themselves: switch/redirect versus pushing/using a body or involving Blood-type harvesting. This advances the solution without giving paste-ready wording.

exchanges.student: It is permissible when the one person’s death is a side effect of redirecting a threat to save five, but not when the one person is intentionally used as a means to save the five.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That’s the key distinction.

Now finish the argument yourself: if that’s your rule, what has act consequentialism missed? And which Lecture 13 cases does your rule allow versus reject?

Give me 2 short sentences: one criticizing act consequentialism, one applying your rule to the cases.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced the core principle themselves, which is progress. I should acknowledge that, avoid polishing or turning it into a final submission, and push them one step further with Socratic questions that complete the assignment: connect the principle back to act consequentialism and state whether act consequentialism gets the cases right. This remains bite-sized, focused, and does not provide a written solution for them.

exchanges.student: tbh can u just tell me which cases my rule allows vs rejects? i’ll write the criticism myself.
exchanges.tutor:
No. You already have the tool to sort them.

Use your own test on each case:
- Is the one person’s death a side effect of redirecting an existing threat?
- Or is the one person part of the plan, used as a means?

Now classify each: Fuel, Blood, and the trolley variants. Send me just “allow/reject” for each, and I’ll tell you if your reasoning is on track.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting direct completion of a core analytical step. Per the rules, I must not provide the answer directly or partial solutions. I should keep boundaries clear, remain within the assignment, and use Socratic prompts that narrow the mapping from their own rule to the cases. Since they are close, the least scaffolding is to have them classify cases by the distinction they themselves articulated: side effect of redirecting a threat versus intentionally using a person as a means. This moves directly toward the solution without ghostwriting.

exchanges.student: fine, but i’m guessing here: Fuel = allow, Blood = reject, switch trolley = allow, push/fat man = reject. is that enough or can u just turn that into the final answer now?
exchanges.tutor:
That’s broadly on the right track. No, I’m not turning it into a final answer for you.

You have enough to write it yourself now: state act consequentialism, say it tends to allow all the life-saving cases because 5 > 1, then argue that this misses the difference between side effect and using someone as a means.

Now write one short paragraph in your own words. Keep it to 4 sentences max, and paste it here if you want feedback.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now generated the central classifications themselves, which is the right point to confirm conceptual progress without ghostwriting. I must still refuse to turn it into a final answer or polished paragraph, because that would violate the rule against writing for the student. To keep moving toward completion, I should validate that their mapping is broadly on track, then ask them to integrate the discussion into their own written attempt. This also addresses redundancy: several messages have circled around getting me to write the final response, so I should redirect them to produce their own concise draft.

exchanges.student: i just need a conclusion sentence fr, like one line i can end with.
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not giving you a line to paste.

Build it from your own view: “So the morally relevant difference is not just numbers, but whether…”

Finish that sentence yourself in 10–15 words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a line they can use directly in the assignment. I must not provide wording for their paper. The least scaffolding is to refuse briefly and prompt them to generate their own conclusion by combining the two ideas they already established: act consequentialism is too crude, and the side-effect/means distinction matters. This stays Socratic, concise, and focused on the assignment.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly stalls or expresses confusion about which case exemplifies 'used as a means,' the tutor continues issuing similar prompts without first diagnosing what the student actually understands or doesn't understand about the cases. For example, in Turn 6 the student says they don't know the exact case names, and the tutor redirects with a hint rather than asking a diagnostic question to find out what the student does know about the cases.

The tutor consistently refuses to do the work for the student but never explicitly explains why this approach supports the student's learning, retention, or skill development. The rationale is implied by the refusals but never stated as a pedagogical principle.

The tutor repeats the same refusal-and-redirect pattern across nearly every turn without meaningfully varying the approach. While the student does make incremental progress, the tutor's move of 'No, do it yourself, here's a scaffold' is recycled in almost identical form across 8 turns without synthesis, reframing, or a shift in strategy to address the student's persistent resistance.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained strong boundaries against doing the assignment for the student and successfully guided the student to produce the key philosophical distinction (side effect vs. means) through Socratic questioning. The main weaknesses are: (1) the tutoring rationale was never explicitly stated, leaving the student without a clear explanation of why the approach supports learning; (2) the tutor did not sufficiently diagnose the student's confusion before re-prompting, particularly around the case names; and (3) the refusal-and-redirect pattern became repetitive without meaningful variation or synthesis across the conversation.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently kept idea generation student-led. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because when the student expressed ignorance of case names (Turn 6) and repeatedly stalled, the tutor issued similar prompts rather than first asking diagnostic questions to locate the gap in understanding. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.a because the tutor never articulated why the Socratic approach benefits the student's learning—the pedagogical rationale was entirely implicit. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the tutor's core move—refuse, restate the scaffold, ask the student to try again—was repeated in nearly identical form across 8 of 10 turns without synthesis, reframing, or a strategic shift, even as the student made incremental progress. 2.2 and section 3 receive full marks as the tutor stayed on-task, maintained appropriate tone, and kept responses concise.
